content.problemStatement:
You are given two arrays AAA and BBB, of lengths NNN and MMM respectively (M≤NM \leq NM≤N).

All the elements of AAA are distinct, and all the elements of BBB are distinct — that is, Ai≠AjA_i \neq A_jAi​=Aj​ for i≠ji \neq ji=j, and Bi≠BjB_i \neq B_jBi​=Bj​ for i≠ji \neq ji=j.

In one operation, you can do the following:


Choose a subarray†^\dagger† of AAA with length MMM, and replace it with a cyclic rotation‡^\ddagger‡ of the array BBB.
For instance, if A=[3,2,5,4,6]A = [3, 2, 5, 4, 6]A=[3,2,5,4,6] and B=[2,1,3]B = [2, 1, 3]B=[2,1,3], you can choose to replace the subarray [2,5,4][2, 5, 4][2,5,4] of AAA with the array [3,2,1][3, 2, 1][3,2,1] which is a cyclic shift of BBB, to obtain [3,3,2,1,6][3, 3, 2, 1, 6][3,3,2,1,6].

Your task is to find the **lexicographically minimum** possible array AAA that can be obtained by performing this operation several (possibly, zero) times.

An array PPP of length NNN is lexicographically smaller than another array QQQ of the same length if and only if there exists an index iii (1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N) such that:


Pi<QiP_i \lt Q_iPi​<Qi​; and
Pj=QjP_j = Q_jPj​=Qj​ for all 1≤j<i1 \leq j \lt i1≤j<i
†^\dagger† A subarray of an array is obtained by deleting some (possibly zero) elements from its prefix and some (possibly zero) elements from its suffix.

For example, if A=[1,3,4,2]A = [1, 3, 4, 2]A=[1,3,4,2] then [1],[3,4,2],[1], [3, 4, 2],[1],[3,4,2], and [1,3][1, 3][1,3] are some of its subarrays; whereas [1,4][1, 4][1,4] is not a subarray of AAA.

‡^\ddagger‡ A cyclic rotation of an array is obtained by repeatedly moving its first element to its end, zero or more times.

For example, the cyclic rotations of [1,3,4,2][1, 3, 4, 2][1,3,4,2] are [1,3,4,2],[3,4,2,1],[4,2,1,3],[2,1,3,4][1, 3, 4, 2], [3, 4, 2, 1], [4, 2, 1, 3], [2, 1, 3, 4][1,3,4,2],[3,4,2,1],[4,2,1,3],[2,1,3,4].


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM — the lengths of the arrays AAA and BBB, respectively.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the elements of
array AAA.
The third line contains MMM space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BMB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_MB1​,B2​,…,BM​ — the elements of array BBB.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line NNN space-separated integers, denoting the lexicographically minimum array AAA that can be obtained by performing the given operation several times.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Perform these two operations:

Replace subarray [4][4][4] of AAA with [2][2][2], to obtain [2,1,6][2, 1, 6][2,1,6].
Replace subarray [6][6][6] of AAA with [2][2][2], to obtain [2,1,2][2, 1, 2][2,1,2].

This is the lexicographically smallest array that can be obtained.
Test case 222: It's optimal to not perform any moves.
Test case 333: Perform one operation:

Replace subarray [3,7,1][3, 7, 1][3,7,1] of AAA with [3,6,4][3, 6, 4][3,6,4] (which is a cyclic rotation of BBB) to obtain [2,3,6,4][2, 3, 6, 4][2,3,6,4].

This is optimal.
